SECURITY AFFAIRS MALWARE NEWSLETTER ROUND 99
Security Affairs newsletter Round 579 by Pierluigi Paganini – INTERNATIONAL EDITION

executive_brief:
The threat landscape has fundamentally shifted this week. Artificial intelligence has compressed the vulnerability lifecycle from months to hours, rendering traditional patching SLAs obsolete. Anthropic's Project Glasswing autonomously identified over 10,000 critical flaws in open-source ecosystems in a single month, forcing regulators like CERT-In to mandate 12-hour remediation windows for internet-facing assets. Defenders are no longer racing against human hackers; they are racing against machine-speed exploitation engines that chain misconfigurations and deploy functional exploits before security teams can triage alerts. This acceleration has created a severe patching bottleneck that attackers are actively weaponizing.

Simultaneously, the software supply chain has collapsed as the new attack surface. Threat actors are abandoning targeted package poisoning in favor of mass CI/CD pipeline compromise, abusing GitHub Actions, OIDC tokens, and IDE extensions to inject credential stealers into thousands of repositories. The Megalodon campaign alone pushed malicious commits to 5,500 public projects, while the GlassWorm and TanStack compromises demonstrate how deeply developer infrastructure is now compromised. Security leaders must treat developer environments as hostile territory and enforce strict micro-perimeters around build pipelines.

The third critical shift is the industrialization of physical and social engineering extortion. Groups like the Silent Ransom Group and ShinyHunters are bypassing hardened networks by walking through doors, impersonating IT support, and leveraging USB drops or social engineering to extract millions of records. Network segmentation is irrelevant when attackers operate from inside the lobby. The convergence of AI-accelerated exploitation, supply chain subversion, and physical intrusion demands a complete architectural overhaul of how enterprises manage trust, patching velocity, and physical access controls.

threat_stories.headline: The 12-Hour Window: AI Compresses the Exploit Lifecycle to Machine Speed
threat_stories.narrative:
Artificial intelligence has shattered the traditional vulnerability management cycle. Anthropic's Project Glasswing, leveraging the Claude Mythos Preview model, scanned over 1,000 open-source projects and identified 10,000 vulnerabilities in a single month, with a 90.6% true-positive rate. This is not theoretical research; it is a demonstration that AI can autonomously discover, analyze, and weaponize flaws faster than human teams can triage them. The patching bottleneck is now the critical vulnerability in enterprise security architectures.

Regulators are responding with emergency mandates. CERT-In issued a directive requiring organizations to patch, mitigate, or isolate critical internet-facing vulnerabilities within 12 hours. This timeline reflects the reality that AI-assisted threat actors automate reconnaissance, exploit development, and deployment, shrinking the defense window from weeks to hours. The median patch time remains 43 days, leaving crown jewel systems exposed to automated exploitation campaigns that chain unpatched flaws across hybrid environments.

The implications are immediate and severe. Enterprises relying on CVSS scores and quarterly patch cycles are operating on obsolete threat models. AI-driven exploitation does not wait for change advisory boards. Defenders must shift from reactive patching to automated compensating controls that deploy within hours of disclosure. The organizations that fail to integrate real-time exploitation intelligence into their CI/CD and network security stacks will face continuous, machine-paced compromise.

timeline.event: Anthropic discloses Project Glasswing findings: 10,000+ OSS vulnerabilities identified in one month
timeline.significance: Demonstrates AI's capacity to autonomously discover and validate flaws at scale, outpacing human triage

timeline.event: CERT-In issues 12-hour patching mandate for internet-facing systems amid AI-assisted attacks
timeline.significance: Regulatory acknowledgment that traditional patching SLAs are obsolete against machine-speed exploitation

timeline.event: CSO Online reports industrialization of exploitation, highlighting fragmented tooling and AI-driven chaining
timeline.significance: Confirms adversaries are autonomously chaining misconfigurations and stale credentials across cloud environments

timeline.event: CERT-In reinforces mandate with tiered remediation SLAs: 1 day critical external, 3 days critical internal
timeline.significance: Establishes enforceable compliance thresholds for real-time threat exposure management

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Internet-facing crown jewels, cloud ecosystems, OT systems, and open-source dependencies face immediate compromise risk. Organizations with patch lag exceeding 12 hours will experience automated exploitation.
threat_stories.action_required: Deploy automated WAF/IPS rules and network isolation scripts that trigger within 1 hour of critical disclosure. Implement Continuous Threat Exposure Management (CTEM) to prioritize remediation based on real exploitability, not CVSS scores. Integrate AI governance directly into CI/CD pipelines as automated deployment gates, not post-hoc reviews.

threat_stories.headline: The Developer Perimeter Collapses: CI/CD and Package Registry Assaults
threat_stories.narrative:
The software supply chain is under sustained, industrialized assault. Attackers have moved beyond targeted package poisoning to mass compromise of developer infrastructure. The Megalodon campaign abused compromised Personal Access Tokens and deploy keys to push malicious commits directly to 5,500 GitHub repositories without pull requests. These commits injected base64-encoded bash payloads into GitHub Actions workflows, harvesting cloud credentials, SSH keys, and OIDC tokens during CI/CD runs.

The supply chain contamination extends to package registries and IDE extensions. The GlassWorm botnet distributed trojanized npm and Python packages alongside malicious VSCode extensions to harvest developer credentials. Simultaneously, attackers abused GitHub Actions trusted-publisher workflows to publish 84 malicious versions of 42 @tanstack packages under legitimate identities. The Nx Console extension and DAEMON Tools installers faced identical supply chain compromises, proving that threat actors are systematically targeting the tools developers trust implicitly.

This campaign architecture bypasses traditional endpoint defenses by operating within authorized CI/CD execution contexts. Malicious workflows run with elevated privileges, exfiltrating secrets to attacker-controlled infrastructure before security teams detect anomalous token usage. The developer workstation and build pipeline are now the primary attack surface for enterprise compromise.

timeline.event: Megalodon campaign pushes malicious commits to 5,500 GitHub repos via compromised PATs
timeline.significance: Demonstrates large-scale CI/CD pipeline abuse for credential and secret exfiltration

timeline.event: Laravel-Lang Composer packages poisoned via Git tag rewriting and malicious fork commits
timeline.significance: Shows attackers manipulating version control metadata to inject cross-platform PHP stealers

timeline.event: GlassWorm botnet infrastructure disrupted after targeting npm/Python/VSCode ecosystems
timeline.significance: Confirms cross-platform supply chain malware campaigns targeting developer toolchains

timeline.event: CISA adds 42 compromised @tanstack packages and Nx Console v18.95.0 to KEV catalog
timeline.significance: Official recognition of widespread package registry and IDE extension compromise

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Software development teams, CI/CD pipelines, and enterprise IT infrastructure face severe credential theft and lateral movement risk. Compromised build artifacts propagate malware downstream to production environments.
threat_stories.action_required: Enforce branch protection rules requiring pull requests and code reviews for all workflow changes. Immediately rotate all PATs, deploy keys, and CI/CD secrets. Implement strict dependency scanning in CI pipelines and restrict PAT scopes to least-privilege access. Audit commit history for forged author identities and base64-encoded payloads.

threat_stories.headline: Trusted Tools Turned Against Defenders: Management Console Compromises
threat_stories.narrative:
Attackers are weaponizing enterprise security and management consoles to bypass endpoint protections and achieve persistent access. FortiClient EMS (CVE-2026-35616) is actively exploited to push the EKZ Infostealer disguised as legitimate Fortinet patches. By compromising the trusted endpoint management infrastructure, threat actors distribute malware across entire networks without triggering user interaction or traditional download alerts. This technique neutralizes EDR solutions by leveraging vendor trust chains.

The exploitation pattern extends to web hosting and collaboration platforms. LiteSpeed cPanel Plugin (CVE-2026-48172) allows arbitrary script execution as root via improper Redis function handling, triggering a CISA emergency directive. PAN-OS GlobalProtect (CVE-2026-0257) faces active authentication bypass campaigns, granting unauthorized VPN access. KnowledgeDeliver LMS (CVE-2026-5426) suffers from hardcoded ASP.NET machineKeys, enabling ViewState deserialization and Godzilla webshell deployment.

These compromises share a critical commonality: they exploit trusted administrative interfaces that operate with elevated privileges. Once breached, these consoles become malware distribution hubs. Defenders cannot rely on perimeter controls or signature-based detection when the management plane itself is compromised. Network segmentation and strict access controls for administrative consoles are now mandatory survival mechanisms.

timeline.event: KnowledgeDeliver zero-day exploited to deploy Godzilla webshell via ViewState deserialization
timeline.significance: Hardcoded machineKeys enable unauthenticated RCE in widely deployed LMS infrastructure

timeline.event: CISA mandates 4-day patching for LiteSpeed cPanel Plugin root RCE vulnerability
timeline.significance: Federal recognition of active exploitation targeting web hosting management interfaces

timeline.event: FortiClient EMS vulnerability actively exploited to push EKZ infostealer disguised as patches
timeline.significance: Trusted endpoint management infrastructure weaponized for enterprise-wide malware distribution

timeline.event: PAN-OS GlobalProtect authentication bypass flaw enters active exploitation phase
timeline.significance: Corporate VPN gateways compromised, granting unauthorized internal network access

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Enterprise IT operations, cybersecurity teams, and web hosting providers face immediate compromise. Management console breaches enable lateral movement, credential theft, and persistent malware distribution.
threat_stories.action_required: Isolate all management consoles to dedicated administrative VLANs with strict IP allowlisting. Disable automatic update features until vendor patch verification is complete. Monitor EMS, cPanel, and VPN logs for anomalous configuration pushes, unauthorized Redis/ViewState requests, and successful logins from untrusted sources.

threat_stories.headline: The Physical Extortion Playbook: Walking Through the Door
threat_stories.narrative:
Extortion groups are abandoning purely digital intrusion methods in favor of physical and social engineering campaigns that bypass network segmentation entirely. The Silent Ransom Group operates by visiting law firms and corporate offices, impersonating IT support to convince employees to insert malicious USB drives or grant remote desktop access. Once physical access is achieved, actors install RATs, escalate privileges, and exfiltrate data using legitimate tools like WinSCP and Rclone. Network defenses are irrelevant when attackers operate from inside the building.

Social engineering remains the primary initial access vector for high-value data theft. ShinyHunters breached Carnival Corporation by deceiving an employee into granting internal IT access, exfiltrating personal data for nearly 6 million customers between April 14 and April 22. The same group targeted Charter Communications, compromising 4.9 million accounts through identical social engineering tactics. These campaigns demonstrate that human manipulation consistently outperforms technical exploitation for large-scale data theft.

The convergence of physical intrusion and social engineering creates an asymmetric advantage for extortion operators. Employees are trained to recognize phishing emails, but rarely trained to verify in-person IT support requests or restrict USB device usage. Organizations must treat physical access as a critical security boundary and implement verification protocols that match the rigor of digital authentication.

timeline.event: FBI warns of Silent Ransom Group visiting US law firms to steal data via USB drops
timeline.significance: Confirms physical intrusion tactics bypassing traditional network security controls

timeline.event: Carnival confirms 6 million record breach after social engineering campaign
timeline.significance: Demonstrates human manipulation as primary vector for enterprise data exfiltration

timeline.event: ShinyHunters leaks Charter Communications data impacting 4.9 million customers
timeline.significance: Extortion group successfully monetizes social engineering breaches across multiple sectors

timeline.event: FBI details in-person data theft attacks targeting legal and financial sectors
timeline.significance: Regulatory warning confirms physical/social engineering as established extortion playbook

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Legal services, hospitality, telecommunications, and enterprise offices face severe data breach and extortion risk. Physical access and social engineering bypass technical security controls.
threat_stories.action_required: Enforce strict physical access controls and mandatory visitor verification protocols. Disable USB ports on publicly accessible and employee workstations. Mandate out-of-band verification for all IT support requests. Deploy EDR solutions tuned for unauthorized remote access tool installations and anomalous data transfer patterns.

threat_stories.headline: State-Sponsored AI Industrialization: GREYVIBE and Nimbus Manticore
threat_stories.narrative:
State-sponsored threat actors are industrializing artificial intelligence to accelerate malware development, phishing lure generation, and infrastructure management. Russia-linked GREYVIBE leverages ChatGPT, Gemini, and Ideogram to develop custom malware like LegionRelay, craft AI-generated phishing campaigns, and manage C2 infrastructure. Despite AI assistance, the group exhibits critical operational security failures, uploading test samples to public scanning services and leaving identifiable development artifacts in deployed payloads.

Iranian-linked Nimbus Manticore follows a similar trajectory, deploying AI-assisted malware variants MiniJunk and MiniFast V2 against aviation and software companies. The group utilizes trojanized Zoom installers, fake Oracle SQL Developer download pages, and .NET AppDomain hijacking to establish persistence. AI tools enable rapid code generation, verbose error handling, and modular RAT capabilities that previously required specialized development resources.

The proliferation of AI-assisted state campaigns lowers the barrier to entry for sophisticated attacks while introducing predictable operational patterns. Both groups demonstrate that AI accelerates offensive capabilities but does not eliminate fundamental OpSec discipline. Defenders can exploit these gaps by monitoring for development artifacts, test sample submissions, and AI-generated lure patterns that lack human refinement.

timeline.event: Nimbus Manticore expands attacks with AI-assisted malware and fake Zoom installers
timeline.significance: Iranian APT demonstrates AI integration into malware development and delivery chains

timeline.event: GREYVIBE attackers use ChatGPT and Gemini to power cyberattacks against Ukraine
timeline.significance: Russia-linked group industrializes AI for custom malware and phishing infrastructure

timeline.event: WithSecure details GREYVIBE's AI-powered campaigns and critical OpSec failures
timeline.significance: Confirms AI acceleration combined with persistent operational security vulnerabilities

timeline.event: CSO Online confirms extensive AI usage in GREYVIBE operations across multiple sectors
timeline.significance: Validates state-sponsored AI industrialization as sustained threat vector

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Ukrainian government and military, aviation sector, software companies, and critical infrastructure face targeted espionage and sabotage campaigns.
threat_stories.action_required: Deploy advanced email filtering with AI-phishing detection and strict web filtering to block malicious domains. Monitor for custom RATs, PowerShell abuse, and cryptocurrency mining activity. Conduct threat hunting for development artifacts and test sample uploads to public scanning services. Enforce endpoint detection tuned for AI-generated malware patterns.

actor_profiles.name: GREYVIBE
actor_profiles.motivation: Espionage, sabotage, and intelligence gathering targeting Ukrainian military, government, and civilian sectors
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Leveraging AI tools (ChatGPT, Gemini, Ideogram) to develop LegionRelay RAT, generate phishing lures, and manage C2 infrastructure. Exhibiting critical OpSec failures including test sample uploads and development artifact leakage.

actor_profiles.name: Nimbus Manticore
actor_profiles.motivation: Espionage and intellectual property theft targeting aviation and software development sectors
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Deploying AI-assisted malware (MiniJunk, MiniFast V2) via trojanized Zoom installers and SEO poisoning. Utilizing .NET AppDomain hijacking and scheduled task manipulation for persistence.

actor_profiles.name: Silent Ransom Group
actor_profiles.motivation: Financial extortion through data theft and physical/social engineering intrusion
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Visiting law firms and corporate offices to impersonate IT support, gaining physical USB access or remote desktop control. Exfiltrating data via WinSCP and Rclone for ransom demands.

actor_profiles.name: ShinyHunters
actor_profiles.motivation: Data extortion and financial theft through social engineering and credential compromise
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Executing social engineering campaigns to breach Carnival Corporation (6M records) and Charter Communications (4.9M records). Monetizing stolen data through ransom demands and dark web sales.

actor_profiles.name: Lazarus Group
actor_profiles.motivation: Financial theft, cryptocurrency targeting, and intelligence gathering
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Deploying RemotePE, a memory-only RAT designed to evade detection against financial and cryptocurrency firms. Utilizing fileless techniques and process injection to maintain persistence.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Improper access control allows unauthenticated attackers to bypass authentication via API endpoints, enabling privilege escalation and remote code execution. Attackers push EKZ Infostealer disguised as legitimate patches.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply Fortinet hotfixes for EMS 7.4.5/7.4.6 immediately or upgrade to version 7.4.7.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Improper handling of Redis enable/disable functions allows any cPanel user to execute arbitrary scripts with root privileges on affected systems.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade to version 2.4.7 or later. Review cPanel logs for suspicious Redis-related API calls.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: SQL injection in the Content API permits unauthenticated attackers to read database contents, steal Admin API keys, and inject malicious JavaScript payloads.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Update to latest patched version immediately. Rotate all Admin API keys and audit database for injected scripts.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Hardcoded ASP.NET machineKey values enable ViewState deserialization attacks, resulting in unauthenticated remote code execution and web shell deployment.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Generate unique, cryptographically strong machine keys per deployment. Apply vendor patch and remove hardcoded keys from web.config.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Authentication bypass vulnerability allows remote attackers to establish unauthorized GlobalProtect VPN connections without valid credentials.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply latest PAN-OS firmware updates immediately. Restrict portal/gateway access to trusted IP ranges.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: SQL injection in Core API bypasses database query sanitization on PostgreSQL-backed sites, enabling arbitrary command execution and privilege escalation.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply critical security patch released May 20, 2026. Federal agencies must remediate by May 27 per CISA BOD 22-01.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Malformed Host headers shift URL parsing boundaries, bypassing host-validation and access control middleware in FastAPI-based AI tools and proxies.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade to Starlette version 1.0.1 or later. Deploy compliant reverse proxies to filter malformed headers.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Cryptographic certificate forgery flaw enables attackers to impersonate legitimate services, undermining encrypted communications and enabling MITM attacks.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply upstream patch immediately. Implement strict certificate pinning and mutual TLS where feasible.

hunting_leads.title: FortiClient EMS Malicious Update Push
hunting_leads.context: Attackers exploit CVE-2026-35616 to push EKZ infostealer disguised as legitimate Fortinet patches, bypassing endpoint protections.
hunting_leads.query: EventCode=4688 AND ProcessName="*forticlient*" AND CommandLine="*powershell*" | where CommandLine contains "download" OR CommandLine contains "invoke"
indicators: Unauthorized PowerShell downloads from EMS consoles

hunting_leads.title: GitHub Actions OIDC Token Exfiltration
hunting_leads.context: Megalodon and TanStack campaigns abuse CI/CD workflows to harvest cloud credentials and OIDC tokens via base64-encoded payloads.
hunting_leads.query: EventSource="GitHub" AND Action="workflow_dispatch" AND Payload contains "base64" | where WorkflowName contains "SysDiag" OR WorkflowName contains "Optimize-Build"

hunting_leads.title: Ghost CMS SQLi & ClickFix Injection
hunting_leads.context: CVE-2026-26980 is exploited to inject malicious JavaScript into databases, fueling automated ad-fraud campaigns across 700+ sites.
hunting_leads.query: WebServerAccessLog AND UrlPath contains "/ghost/api/v3/content/" AND RequestMethod="POST" AND StatusCode=200 | where RequestBody contains "UNION SELECT" OR RequestBody contains "OR 1=1"
indicators: Injected JS payloads in content tables

hunting_leads.title: Silent Ransom Group Physical/Remote Access
hunting_leads.context: Actors impersonate IT support to install RATs or use USB drops, bypassing network segmentation through physical presence.
hunting_leads.query: ProcessName="*anydesk.exe" OR ProcessName="*rustdesk.exe" OR ProcessName="*zohomeeting*" OR DeviceType="USB" | where User NOT IN (AllowedITSupportUsers)
indicators: WinSCP/Rclone data transfers to external IPs

hunting_leads.title: GREYVIBE AI-Generated Phishing Lures
hunting_leads.context: Russia-linked group uses AI for custom malware and phishing targeting Ukraine, exhibiting OpSec failures in lure generation.
hunting_leads.query: EmailGatewayLog AND SenderDomain in ("*.xyz", "*.top") AND AttachmentType="*.zip" | where Subject contains "CAPTCHA" OR Subject contains "Verification"

{'summary': 'Real-time MFA/OTP interception and credential harvesting via PhaaS platforms', 'severity': 'high', 'affected_products': ['SMS/OTP-based MFA systems', 'Digital Wallet Provisioning Services', 'RCS/iMessage Clients', 'Web Browsers'], 'complexity': 'low', 'cvss_score': 0.0, 'workarounds': ['Enforce FIDO2/WebAuthn security keys for critical accounts', 'Delay or limit SMS/OTP delivery frequency', 'Implement step-up verification for digital wallet provisioning', 'Use email or voice calls for out-of-band verification instead of SMS/RCS'], 'references': ['Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) Analysis: 2 PhaaS 2 Furious', 'Article: 2 PhaaS 2 Furious: The Evolution of Chinese-language Phishing Services (2026-05-25)'], 'integrity_impact': 'high', 'description': "Phishing-as-a-Service (PhaaS) platforms enable attackers to bypass multifactor authentication (MFA) through real-time interception. When a victim enters credentials on a dynamically generated phishing page, the data is instantly relayed to an attacker's live administration panel. The attacker simultaneously triggers an OTP request on their own device, capturing the code seconds before expiration. Stolen credentials and OTPs are then used to provision payment cards into digital wallets on attacker-controlled devices, enabling tokenization for unauthorized transactions. Delivery leverages end-to-end encrypted channels like RCS and iMessage to bypass carrier security filters. AI-powered tools (e.g., Puppeteer) generate unique, high-fidelity phishing pages to evade signature-based detection, while anti-bot verification screens hinder automated security analysis.", 'attack_vector': 'network', 'privileges_required': 'none', 'exploit_maturity': 'high', 'availability_impact': 'low', 'remediation': 'Transition authentication infrastructure to phishing-resistant standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn. Implement risk-based authentication and device fingerprinting during digital wallet provisioning. Deploy on-device security controls and link inspection for RCS/iMessage. Utilize AI-driven behavioral analytics to detect anomalous login patterns and rapid OTP usage.', 'exploit_available': 'yes', 'cve_id': 'N/A', 'cvss_vector': 'N/A', 'user_interaction': 'required', 'scope': 'changed', 'confidentiality_impact': 'high', 'published_date': '2026-05-25', 'discovery_date': ''}
statistics.key_changes: AI-driven vulnerability discovery has compressed the exploit lifecycle to hours, forcing regulatory mandates for 12-hour patching windows on internet-facing assets. Supply chain attacks have shifted from targeted package poisoning to mass CI/CD pipeline compromise via GitHub Actions abuse and OIDC token theft.
emerging_trends: Physical intrusion blended with social engineering for extortion (Silent Ransom Group)
AI agents and LLMs becoming direct attack surfaces (ChatGPhish, prompt injection)
Compromised endpoint management consoles used to push malware (FortiClient EMS, PAN-OS)
State actors industrializing AI for malware development and lure generation
declining_threats: Traditional credential stuffing as primary initial access (surpassed by vulnerability exploitation)
Static phishing templates (replaced by AI-generated, real-time interception PhaaS)
Unpatched legacy internet-facing services (due to accelerated regulatory SLAs)
